Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Donald Griffin

1915 – 2003

I am taking “Donald Griffin” to mean Donald R.

I am taking “Donald Griffin” to mean Donald R. Griffin, the American zoologist and founder of cognitive ethology. The most defensible reconstruction of Griffin’s philosophy of mind is this: he is a comparative biological naturalist about consciousness. Minds are not ghostly substances; they are subjective episodes of feeling, perceiving, remembering, desiring, anticipating, intending, choosing, and sometimes reflecting, produced by nervous systems and studied by inference from behavior, communication, and neural continuity. But he is also an anti-reductionist in the methodological sense: he rejects the idea that science becomes more rigorous by pretending that subjective experience does not exist. For Griffin, consciousness is a biological phenomenon whose distribution across species is a scientific question, not something to be settled in advance by human exceptionalism, language ideology, Cartesian theology, or behaviorist taboo.

Griffin’s thought is not a theology of the soul, but it has substantial theological force. His work challenges any doctrine, religious or secular, that treats human mindedness as an absolute metaphysical island. If one keeps the word “soul,” the most Griffin-compatible use is not “immaterial substance separable from the body,” but “living subject of experience”: an animal that has felt, desired, feared, intended, remembered, suffered, or enjoyed. That is not a minor semantic point; it changes the moral and metaphysical landscape.

1. Griffin’s project: making animal consciousness scientifically admissible

Griffin’s authority mattered because he was not a casual sentimentalist about animals. Carolyn Ristau’s 2024 précis describes him as co-discoverer of bat sonar, founder of the field of echolocation, researcher on bird migration, and founder of cognitive ethology, understood as the study of animal awareness and mental experiences. Ristau also notes that Griffin faced hostile opposition in each of these domains, including in his later arguments for animal consciousness.

The animating problem in The Question of Animal Awareness is not merely “Are animals intelligent?” but “Do nonhuman animals consciously think about objects, events, future actions, and themselves?” Griffin says that behavioral science had often assumed that only humans possess conscious thinking, planning, and “awareness,” but that the correctness of this assumption is crucial for understanding what is and is not unique to human beings. He therefore asks how objective and verifiable data about animal awareness might be gathered.

His program was interdisciplinary from the start. The revised edition of The Question of Animal Awareness explicitly draws from ethology, evolutionary biology, comparative psychology, linguistics, and philosophy, because Griffin thought no single discipline owned the problem of mind. This is important: Griffin is not simply importing human introspection into zoology. He is attempting to build a comparative science of subjectivity.

The canonical statement is his 1978 article “Prospects for a cognitive ethology.” There he argues that animal social behavior, discrimination learning, and especially communicative behavior are versatile enough to call into question the customary denial that animals have mental experiences comparable in some respects to ours. He explicitly mentions mental images, self-awareness, intentions concerning future actions, and the inhibitory effect of the behavioristic Zeitgeist. Crucially, he adds that reopening these questions “requires no departure from the materialistic approach” to biology and behavioral science.

So the project is simultaneously conservative and revolutionary. It is conservative because Griffin keeps methodological naturalism: study observable behavior, seek objective tests, use controls, compare nervous systems, avoid wild speculation. It is revolutionary because he says the object of study must include subjective experience itself, not merely external stimulus-response patterns or nonconscious information processing.

2. The key concepts: mind, consciousness, awareness, self-awareness, soul

Griffin’s “mind” is broader than abstract reasoning. It includes perceptual consciousness, emotional feeling, desire, intention, memory, anticipation, belief-like states, and choice. In the University of Chicago excerpt from Animal Minds, Griffin says cognitive ethology must include subjective feelings as well as cognition, because emotional feelings are central to human mental experience and very likely to animal mental experience too. He criticizes the restriction of cognitive ethology to nonconscious information processing as an “obsolete relic of behaviorism.”

This gives us the first major thesis: for Griffin, mind is affective-intentional subjectivity. It is not just computation, not just information processing, and not just intelligence. A being has a mind in the relevant sense when there is something it is like for that being to perceive, want, fear, seek, avoid, remember, or choose.

His concept of consciousness is similarly modest but profound. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes Griffin’s definition of consciousness as “the subjective state of feeling or thinking about objects and events,” and notes that he emphasized behavioral flexibility and versatility as evidence for consciousness. This definition matters because it does not make linguistic self-report the essence of consciousness. Consciousness is not first of all the ability to say “I am conscious”; it is the felt or experienced directedness of an organism toward objects and events.

Griffin also distinguishes perceptual consciousness from reflective consciousness. In Animal Minds, he follows Natsoulas in distinguishing basic awareness of something from awareness of one’s own mental acts. He calls the first “perceptual consciousness” and the second “reflective consciousness.” Perceptual consciousness may include immediate perception, memory, anticipation, and imagining absent or nonexistent objects. Reflective consciousness involves awareness that one is thinking or feeling.

This distinction is central to Griffin’s philosophy of mind. Many debates about animal consciousness become confused because critics use “consciousness” to mean reflective introspection and then conclude that animals are not conscious because they cannot prove introspective self-awareness in a human-like manner. Griffin’s reply is that perceptual consciousness is already consciousness, and its existence and distribution are important even if reflective consciousness is rarer or harder to detect.

Self-awareness, for Griffin, is not an all-or-nothing human possession. He does not claim that all animals possess robust autobiographical self-consciousness. But he argues that if an animal is conscious of its own actions, body, fear, enjoyment, and goal-directed behavior, it becomes artificial to deny every form of self-awareness. In The Question of Animal Awareness, he warns that if one grants an animal broad awareness of companions, signals, surroundings, and bodily inputs while denying any self-awareness, one risks defining self-awareness as a strange “hole” in the animal’s experienced universe.

The word “soul” is not Griffin’s central technical term. His standard vocabulary is awareness, consciousness, mental experience, subjective feeling, thought, intention, and animal mind. But the concept of soul becomes relevant when his thought is placed in theological or metaphysical context. In Animal Minds, discussing James Savage’s claim that animals have “souls,” Griffin glosses the word in that context as meaning something close to conscious mental states rather than anything necessarily immaterial or spiritual.

That passage is the key to a Griffinian use of “soul.” If “soul” means an immaterial, separable, uniquely human substance, Griffin does not build his science on it. If “soul” means the presence of living subjectivity—feeling, desire, purpose, consciousness—then Griffin’s work supports a broad attribution of soul-like life to many animals.

3. Griffin’s ontology: materialism without eliminativism

Griffin’s ontology is naturalistic but not eliminativist. He does not need consciousness to be outside the physical world. In the 1978 Behavioral and Brain Sciences article, he says the study of animal mental experience requires no departure from materialistic biology. In Animal Minds, he likewise approaches animal mentality from the viewpoint of a materialist who assumes mental experiences result from physiological processes in central nervous systems, while insisting that these processes are not “tangible objects” or “immaterial essences.”

This is a subtle position. Griffin rejects two extremes. Against Cartesian or vitalist dualism, he says consciousness need not be treated as an immaterial substance or divine intervention. Against behaviorist or eliminative reductionism, he says consciousness should not be ignored merely because it is private, difficult to measure, or not yet reducible to a mechanism.

His preferred analogy is scientific realism about unobservables. In The Question of Animal Awareness, he notes that many sciences have progressed by positing entities or processes not directly observable at first, but inferred from their causes and effects: gravitation, electric potentials, magnetic fields, atoms, genes, hormones, nerve impulses, and so on. Conscious experience, on Griffin’s view, should be treated in the same broad epistemic family: not directly visible from the outside, but inferable from converging evidence.

This makes Griffin a realist about consciousness. He does not treat mental terms as mere shorthand for behavior. If an animal fears, desires, chooses, intends, or remembers, those are not just decorative words. They may identify real subjective states that help organize behavior. But he is not saying such states are final explanations. He repeatedly insists that to recognize a mental state is not to stop looking for neural, developmental, ecological, and evolutionary mechanisms.

The result is best called biological naturalism about animal subjectivity. Conscious states are biological events or processes, generated by nervous systems, shaped by evolution, and causally relevant to action. They are not metaphysical ghosts, but neither are they dispensable fictions.

4. Mental causation: consciousness as a local cause of behavior

Griffin is particularly concerned to resist epiphenomenalism—the idea that consciousness is a passive by-product with no influence on behavior. In Animal Minds, he says conscious subjective mental experience is useful because it helps organisms select appropriate behavior; mental experiences are “local causes” of behavior, though themselves influenced by prior events. He describes animals as actors who choose what to do, within constraints, especially when they solve novel challenges in versatile ways.

This does not mean animals have unconstrained free will in a metaphysically extravagant sense. Griffin’s claim is more modest and more scientifically relevant: conscious thought can be part of the proximate causal organization of behavior. It can help an animal compare possibilities, remember dangers, anticipate outcomes, communicate about absent objects, or adjust tactics under novel conditions.

His evolutionary argument follows from this. If consciousness helps animals cope with changing situations, it may have adaptive value. In The Question of Animal Awareness, Griffin argues that conscious awareness may be an efficient way for complex animals to deal with spatial and temporal patterns, anticipate future enjoyment or injury, and adapt behavior to probable future events. He explicitly suggests that it may be more parsimonious to posit limited conscious awareness than to posit cumbersome chains of reflexes and internal motivational states.

This is one of Griffin’s strongest philosophical moves. He reverses the usual appeal to parsimony. Critics often say that attributing consciousness to animals is unparsimonious. Griffin replies that, in many cases, a modest conscious-state explanation may be simpler than an elaborate nonconscious mechanism invented solely to avoid mental vocabulary.

5. The evidential architecture: how animal minds can be studied

Griffin’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from his epistemology. He knows that animal consciousness cannot be read off behavior with certainty. His method is probabilistic, cumulative, and comparative.

The three major evidential routes are neural correlates, behavioral versatility under novel challenges, and communication. Ristau summarizes Griffin’s proposed evidence in exactly these terms: neural correlates of consciousness, versatile behavior in response to novel challenges, and animal communication as a possible “window” on animal minds. Griffin and Gayle Speck’s 2004 review gives the same structure: no uniquely human consciousness-producing structure has been found; novel, flexible behavior gives suggestive evidence; and animal communication often reports subjective experiences.

First, neural continuity matters. Griffin does not argue that every nervous system is conscious. His point is narrower: if human consciousness arises from neural processes, and no consciousness-producing structure has been found that is uniquely human, then there is no principled basis for restricting consciousness to humans in advance.

Second, behavioral versatility matters. Fixed reflexes are weak evidence for consciousness. Flexible, context-sensitive, innovative, goal-directed responses to new problems are stronger evidence. Griffin is especially interested in cases where the animal appears to compare alternatives, remember absent objects or events, anticipate outcomes, or modify behavior in light of new information.

Third, communication matters most because it is the closest analogue to how we know other human minds. In the 1978 article, Griffin says communicative behavior is especially promising because it may serve a function analogous to human language, which gives us our best evidence about the mental experiences of other people. In The Question of Animal Awareness, he calls animal communication a “window” through which we may learn at least something about animal mental experiences.

This does not require animal language to be identical to human language. Griffin rejects the assumption that only human-style syntax can disclose mind. A honeybee dance, vervet alarm call, ape gesture, parrot request, or other communicative act can be evidence if it reliably conveys information about objects, events, desires, dangers, or intentions.

Modern philosophy of animal consciousness has, in effect, adopted a Griffin-like marker approach, even when it avoids his terminology. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that animal consciousness science is still early and often proceeds by searching for fallible behavioral and neural markers rather than waiting for a complete theory of consciousness. Good markers do not prove consciousness, but they raise its probability and guide research. This is very close to Griffin’s cumulative evidential method.

6. Evolutionary continuity and the anti-exceptionalist argument

The deepest principle of Griffin’s thought is evolutionary continuity. He does not say humans and other animals are mentally identical. He says that if anatomy, physiology, biochemistry, learning, and behavior exhibit evolutionary continuity, it is suspicious to make consciousness a sudden absolute discontinuity.

In The Question of Animal Awareness, Griffin frames full acceptance of the evolutionary continuity of mental experience as an unfinished phase of the Darwinian revolution. He explicitly links resistance to animal awareness with residues of cultural heritage and faith in human uniqueness and superiority, while noting that few behavioral scientists explicitly reject animal awareness on religious grounds.

This is one of the places where theological interpretation must be taken seriously. Griffin is not simply accusing religion of blocking science. His point is more interesting: explicit religious doctrine may not be the direct reason modern scientists deny animal consciousness, but older structures of human exceptionalism can persist as cultural residues even within secular science.

He presses the same issue when discussing arguments that humans and animals must differ radically in kind in order to preserve human dignity or rights. Griffin reports Mortimer Adler’s worry that if animals differ from humans only in degree, we may lose the moral basis for treating animals differently, and perhaps even endanger the basis of human dignity. Griffin’s response is not to avoid the inquiry but to insist that these consequences make it more important to investigate human-animal cognition and communication accurately.

This is philosophically decisive. Griffin refuses to let moral anxiety determine metaphysics. If human dignity depends on a false picture of animal mindlessness, then the problem lies not with animal consciousness but with the theory of dignity.

He also rejects circular language exceptionalism. He asks whether we must reject evolutionary continuity to preserve a “gut feeling” of human superiority, and he challenges arguments that assume language is uniquely human and then treat all animal communication as incomparable by definition.

The extracted principle is this: no absolute psychological discontinuity should be posited unless the evidence requires it. Enormous differences of degree are compatible with continuity. Griffin does not flatten humans into animals; he animalizes humans in the Darwinian sense. We are animals whose minds are exceptionally elaborate, not beings whose mindedness belongs to a separate ontological order.

7. Griffin’s anti-behaviorism: against both anthropomorphism and anthropodenial

Griffin’s position is sometimes caricatured as naïve anthropomorphism. That is not fair. He repeatedly warns against overinterpretation. In The Question of Animal Awareness, he discusses the danger of relaxing critical standards, notes the Clever Hans problem, and says researchers must avoid both exaggerated attributions of complex mental activity and the reductionist claim that animals have no mental experiences or that such experiences are inaccessible.

His critique of Morgan’s canon is especially important. Morgan’s canon is often invoked to prohibit mentalistic interpretation where simpler explanations are available. Griffin replies that Morgan did not intend to deny mental experience in humans and animals; rather, the canon was a cautionary rule for responsible interpretation. Griffin says the view that any attribution of animal mental experience is anthropomorphic may confuse scientific caution with unscientific feelings of human superiority.

This gives Griffin a balanced methodological ethic. Do not project elaborate human thoughts into animals without evidence. But do not project mindlessness either. A reflex-only explanation can be just as ideologically motivated as an overgenerous anthropomorphic one.

A modern term often used for the latter error is “anthropodenial”: the refusal to recognize human-like traits in animals when the evidence supports them. Griffin did not rely on the term as his central category, but his critique anticipates it. His point is that both anthropomorphism and anthropodenial are distortions. The scientist’s task is not to protect human uniqueness but to infer the most plausible explanation.

8. The theological significance: soul, human uniqueness, and moral status

Griffin’s work has at least five theological implications.

First, it weakens Cartesian animal-machine theology. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Descartes treated animals as mere automata or machines lacking conscious experience, while Darwinian continuity later challenged that picture. Griffin’s entire project is a scientific rejection of the animal-machine picture. He does not need to refute every form of dualism, but he makes it increasingly difficult to maintain a sharp division between human ensouled subjectivity and animal mechanism.

Second, Griffin’s thought is compatible with an Aristotelian or Thomistic language of animal souls more than with a Cartesian denial of animal consciousness. Aristotle’s psychology treats soul as the actuality or form of a living body, with plants, animals, and humans possessing different psychic capacities; animals have perception, while humans additionally have intellect. Aquinas likewise treats soul as the substantial form of living things and says this does not automatically ascribe special spirituality to plants and animals; animals remain material beings. In that kind of framework, Griffin’s evidence would strengthen the case for richly experiential animal “sensitive souls,” even if it does not settle questions about rational or immortal souls.

Third, Griffin pressures doctrines of human uniqueness to become more precise. If “unique” means that humans possess unparalleled symbolic, linguistic, historical, theological, or cultural capacities, Griffin need not deny it. If “unique” means that no other animals feel, think, intend, remember, or consciously experience anything, Griffin’s work is directly opposed.

Fourth, Griffin relocates dignity. He does not argue that animal consciousness destroys human dignity. He suggests instead that human dignity should not depend on denying the mental reality of other animals. This has theological importance: a theology of creation, stewardship, kinship, or creaturely fellowship can absorb Griffin more easily than a theology built around absolute psychic discontinuity.

Fifth, Griffin’s view expands moral relevance. Allen and Bekoff note that Griffin’s The Question of Animal Awareness sought to revolutionize the science of animal behavior by placing animal consciousness at the center of cognitive ethology, and that this was ethically relevant because rejecting behaviorist prohibitions against subjective states offered support for the claim that animals are conscious in morally important ways.

This does not mean moral status depends only on high intelligence. In fact, one Griffinian correction is to distinguish consciousness from cleverness. A cognitively simple animal may still suffer. Conversely, a cognitively impressive animal may not be morally relevant merely because it solves puzzles, but because there is something it is like to be that animal. Spanish and German Animal Ethics discussions make this distinction explicit: they argue that animal cognition has received more attention than sentience, while the morally central issue is consciousness or sentience itself.

9. Non-English reception: what appears outside the Anglo framing

The Spanish reception represented by El País emphasizes Griffin as the founder of the modern study of animal reasoning and consciousness. It presents his cognitive ethology as a taboo-breaking intervention: animals are not mere automata, but thinking beings, though they may think about different things and in different ways from humans. It also notes the standard criticism that cognitive ethology is anecdotal, subjective, and anthropomorphic.

The Italian reception in La Nuova Ecologia sharpens the philosophical diagnosis. It says Griffin denounced the twentieth-century slide from methodological caution or agnosticism into dogmatic, anthropocentric denial of animal mental life. It also frames his work as refusing to place mind outside biology: if evolutionary continuity applies only to anatomy but not cognition, then human cognitive capacities become isolated exceptions. This Italian framing is especially useful because it sees Griffin not merely as “pro-animal consciousness,” but as a critic of a hidden metaphysical dualism inside allegedly scientific reductionism.

A German-language Swiss ethics publication describes Griffin as the father of cognitive ethology and says his primary interest was not merely learning, intelligence, communication, or thought, but the question of animal consciousness; it glosses his project as opening “a window to the spirit/mind of animals” through complex flexible behavior and animal communication. The German term Geist is significant: it can mean mind, spirit, or intellect depending on context. That makes visible something English sometimes hides: Griffin’s question is not only cognitive but spiritual-metaphysical in resonance, even when his method is naturalistic.

A French OpenEdition article situates Griffin’s 1976 The Question of Animal Awareness as the manifesto-like beginning of cognitive ethology and argues that Griffin’s call, despite criticism, was heard insofar as animal cognition now integrates experimental and behavioral-ecological approaches to mental representations and environmental adaptability. The French discussion frames Griffin within “la philosophie de l’esprit des animaux,” the philosophy of animal minds, and emphasizes methodological questions about mental representations, explanation, and the relation between cognitive science and biology.

Across these non-English receptions, a pattern emerges. English-language discussions often focus on whether Griffin’s evidence meets scientific standards. Spanish, Italian, German, and French discussions more often foreground the anthropocentric, metaphysical, or philosophical stakes: automaton versus subject, biological machine versus minded creature, caution versus dogmatic denial, cognition versus sentience, and animal mind as a genuine object of philosophy.

10. Criticisms and limits

A rigorous reconstruction must also state the limits of Griffin’s program.

First, consciousness is hard to infer. Griffin knows this, but the problem remains. Flexible behavior may indicate consciousness, but it may also be produced by nonconscious cognition. Communication may express subjective states, but it may also be behaviorally controlled in ways that do not require the specific conscious contents we infer.

Second, cognitive ethology has never become an uncontested discipline. Allen and Bekoff warn ethicists not to treat cognitive ethology as a mature field whose results can be cited without controversy. They stress that there remains a long way to go before we have an adequate database for claims about the taxonomic distribution of cognitive capacities relevant to ethics.

Third, Griffin’s evidential standard is intentionally probabilistic. Griffin and Speck explicitly say that no single piece of evidence provides absolute proof of consciousness; the accumulation of evidence increases the likelihood that some animals have simple conscious thoughts and feelings. This is not a weakness if understood properly. It is how much of comparative biology proceeds. But it means Griffin’s conclusions should be stated in degrees of probability, not as uniform certainty.

Fourth, there is a distinction between consciousness, cognition, sentience, intelligence, self-awareness, and reflective thought. Griffin sometimes uses “consciousness” broadly enough that critics worry about conceptual looseness. Contemporary animal cognition research often separates these categories more sharply. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that cognitive ethology began as a field focused on animal consciousness, but Griffin’s use of consciousness also included beliefs, intentions, self-awareness, deception, theory of mind, perception, memory, spatial cognition, language, and tool use; today, many researchers use different labels and integrate experimental, ethological, biological, and anthropological approaches.

Fifth, theological use of Griffin must be disciplined. Griffin does not prove animal immortality, reincarnation, rational souls, divine image, or the absence of a uniquely human vocation. He does show that denying animal subjectivity is scientifically and philosophically unstable. Theology can build on that, but should not pretend Griffin has settled every doctrinal question.

11. The extracted Griffinian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Griffin can be stated in the following theses. 1. The reality thesis: conscious experience is real. It is not merely a behaviorist fiction, linguistic convenience, or folk-psychological projection. Fear, desire, pain, enjoyment, intention, memory, anticipation, and perceptual awareness can be genuine features of animal life. 2. The biological thesis: consciousness is a biological phenomenon produced by nervous systems. It need not be immaterial, vitalistic, or divinely inserted to be real. Griffin’s science proceeds within materialistic biology, while refusing to reduce away subjective experience. 3. The continuity thesis: human consciousness is exceptional in richness, abstraction, language, and reflective complexity, but it is not an absolute discontinuity from animal mind. Evolutionary continuity should be the default unless strong evidence demands a break. Griffin treats acceptance of mental continuity as an unfinished Darwinian revolution. 4. The plurality thesis: there is no single animal mind. Animal consciousness differs by species, sensory world, ecology, body, social life, and behavioral repertoire. What it is like to be a bat, bee, crow, parrot, dog, fish, or chimpanzee may differ profoundly from what it is like to be human. This difference is not evidence of absence. 5. The affective thesis: emotion is central, not peripheral. Subjective feelings, desires, aversions, fears, and preferences are not mere decorations on cognition; they are often the heart of mindedness. 6. The intentionality thesis: animal minds are about things. They are directed toward food, mates, dangers, companions, places, remembered events, desired goals, and possible futures. Griffin’s view is not merely that animals feel, but that they may experience objects and events as meaningful for action. 7. The causal thesis: conscious experience can help organize behavior. It is a local cause, especially where animals face novelty, uncertainty, flexible choice, and goal-directed action. 8. The evidential thesis: other minds are known by inference, not direct inspection. This is true for humans as well as animals. The relevant evidence is cumulative: neural similarity, behavioral versatility, communication, preference, learning, memory, planning, and ecological context. 9. The communication thesis: animal communication can function as a partial window into animal experience. It is not a perfect window, and animal signals need not be human language; but communicative behavior can disclose what an animal perceives, wants, fears, remembers, or intends. 10. The anti-taboo thesis: difficulty of measurement is not a reason to deny existence. Griffin’s list of formerly taboo terms—feeling, awareness, mental experience, mind, thought, choice, consciousness—shows that scientific respectability often tracks disciplinary fashion as much as evidence. 11. The middle-way methodological thesis: avoid both sentimental anthropomorphism and reductionist anthropodenial. Griffin explicitly warns against overinterpretation while also rejecting the conventional claim that animal mental experiences do not exist or are inaccessible in principle. 12. The naturalized soul thesis: if “soul” means an immaterial, uniquely human substance, Griffin’s philosophy does not require it. If “soul” means the presence of conscious subjectivity in a living creature, then Griffin supports animal souls in a broad, naturalized sense. His own gloss of “soul” in the Savage discussion points toward conscious mental states rather than necessarily immaterial or spiritual entities. 13. The ethical thesis: the more probable animal consciousness becomes, the harder it is to treat animals as mere instruments. Griffin does not derive a full animal-rights theory, but his work supplies the philosophy of mind that many animal ethics arguments require: animals matter morally because their lives may matter to them.

Final synthesis

Griffin’s philosophy of mind can be named “comparative affective biological naturalism.” It is comparative because the mind must be studied across species rather than defined around the human case. It is affective because feeling, desire, fear, enjoyment, and preference are central. It is biological because consciousness is an evolved function of living nervous systems. It is naturalistic because it does not require immaterial souls. But it is not reductive in the behaviorist sense, because it treats subjective experience as a real target of science.

Theologically, Griffin is best read as a destroyer of the animal-machine picture, not as a destroyer of all human distinctiveness. He does not abolish the possibility of a special human vocation, rationality, or theological status. He does make it intellectually and morally dangerous to base that status on the denial that other animals are subjects. A Griffinian theology of mind would therefore say: human beings may be distinctive, but they are not the only creatures with interiority; animals may not possess human rational self-consciousness, but many plausibly possess felt life; and the language of “soul,” if used responsibly, must not be allowed to erase the conscious lives of nonhuman creatures.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
